skill.name: meta-forest-binary-plot
skill.description: Generate meta-analysis forest plots for binary classification data. Input is a CSV file containing study names, event counts and sample sizes for experimental and control groups. Output includes forest plot PNG and data table CSV.
skill.summary: Generate publication-ready forest plots from binary classification study data with pooled effect size estimates and heterogeneity statistics.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: All 50 static findings were evaluated and determined to be FALSE POSITIVES. The 17 'Weak cryptographic algorithm' findings are misidentified argparse.ArgumentParser calls (no crypto operations exist in the codebase). The 30 'Shell backtick execution' findings are R column-name quoting and markdown code formatting. The 1 'Python os file operations' finding at line 288 of forest_binary.py is legitimate output directory creation for generated files. The legacy audit confirms a 91/100 score with 'Production Ready' grade and all veto gates passing. No prompt injection attempts detected.

high_findings.title: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm - FALSE POSITIVE (ArgumentParser misidentified)
high_findings.description: Static scanner flagged argparse.ArgumentParser() calls as 'weak cryptographic algorithm' in 17 locations. No cryptographic operations exist in this codebase. The scanner pattern-matched on 'parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()' which is a standard Python CLI argument parser, not a cryptographic function. All instances are legitimate command-line argument definitions.

medium_findings.title: Shell Backtick Execution - FALSE POSITIVE (R column quoting / markdown formatting)
medium_findings.description: Static scanner flagged 30 locations as 'Ruby/shell backtick execution'. In forest_binary.R line 119, backticks are R syntax for quoting column names containing special characters (column 'P value (pooled)'). In SKILL.md, backticks are standard markdown inline code formatting used to display example bash commands. Neither constitutes actual command execution. The R script uses the `meta` package for legitimate statistical computation.

medium_findings.title: Python OS File Operations - FALSE POSITIVE (output directory creation)
medium_findings.description: Flagged os.makedirs(output_dir) at forest_binary.py:288. This is legitimate file system usage to create the output directory for generated forest plot PNG and CSV files. The skill's documented purpose is to generate output files. The output_dir is derived from user-provided argument or input CSV path, not from arbitrary system paths.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL - FALSE POSITIVE (source attribution)
low_findings.description: Flagged URL in SKILL.md line 7. This is a source attribution link pointing to the public GitHub repository of the skill (github.com/aipoch/medical-research-skills). It is documentation, not a network call or data exfiltration endpoint.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance - FALSE POSITIVE (documentation text)
low_findings.description: Flagged as 'System reconnaissance' in 4 locations within audit_result_v2.json and SKILL.md. These are regular documentation text and audit result JSON fields (code usability notes, parameter documentation). No system reconnaissance behavior present.

content.user_title: Generate Meta-Analysis Forest Plots from Binary Data
content.value_statement: Creating forest plots for meta-analysis of binary data requires specialized statistical knowledge and complex scripting. This skill automates the process by reading a standard CSV format and generating publication-ready forest plots with combined effect sizes and heterogeneity metrics.
actual_capabilities: Read CSV files containing study names, event counts, and sample sizes for experimental and control groups
Calculate odds ratios with 95% confidence intervals for each individual study
Compute pooled effect sizes using random-effects meta-analysis with inverse-variance weighting
Generate publication-ready forest plot PNG images with study-level and summary data
Export complete numerical results as structured CSV data tables
Calculate heterogeneity statistics including I-squared and Tau-squared values
limitations: Requires input data in a specific CSV column format with exact column names
Only supports binary outcome data (event counts), not continuous variables or mean differences
Does not perform subgroup analysis, meta-regression, or publication bias assessment
R and Python statistical packages must be pre-installed in the execution environment

use_cases.title: Systematic Review Data Visualization
use_cases.description: Convert extracted study data from a systematic literature review into professional forest plot graphics

use_cases.title: Clinical Trial Meta-Analysis
use_cases.description: Aggregate and visualize binary outcomes such as mortality or adverse events across multiple clinical trials

use_cases.title: Evidence Synthesis Reporting
use_cases.description: Generate standardized forest plots with heterogeneity statistics for academic publications and regulatory submissions

prompt_templates.title: Quick Forest Plot from CSV
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a forest plot from the CSV file at {csv_path} using the meta-forest-binary-plot skill
prompt_templates.scenario: User has a CSV ready and needs a quick visualization

prompt_templates.title: Forest Plot with Custom Outcome Label
prompt_templates.prompt: Run the meta-forest-binary-plot skill on {csv_path} with outcome name '{outcome_name}' and save outputs to {output_dir}
prompt_templates.scenario: User wants to specify the outcome measure being analyzed

prompt_templates.title: Python Fallback Execution
prompt_templates.prompt: Execute the Python forest_binary.py script on the validated CSV at {csv_path} specifying outcome '{outcome_name}' and output directory '{output_dir}'
prompt_templates.scenario: R environment is unavailable, use the Python fallback script

prompt_templates.title: Full Validation and Visualization Pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: First validate the CSV file at {csv_path} using extract_criteria.py, then generate a forest plot using the R script at scripts/forest_binary.R with outcome '{outcome_name}'. Save all outputs to {output_dir}
prompt_templates.scenario: User needs data validation before visualization

output_examples.input: A CSV file with columns: study, group1_Events, group1_sample_size, group2_Events, group2_sample_size for 6 studies on a new treatment
output_examples.output: Forest plot PNG image with 6 study rows showing odds ratios and confidence intervals, plus a diamond shape representing the pooled effect estimate at the bottom

output_examples.input: CSV data for 5 clinical trials on a cholesterol drug with outcome labeled as 'Mortality'
output_examples.output: Two files: Binary_forest_Mortality.png (forest plot) and Binary_forest_Mortality.csv (data table with study-level OR, CI, weights, and heterogeneity statistics)

best_practices: Always validate input CSV data quality before running the visualization scripts to avoid execution errors
Use the R script as the primary execution path for best statistical accuracy and standard meta-analysis methods
Specify an explicit output directory to keep generated files organized and easy to locate
anti_patterns: Do not skip data validation - confirm all required columns are present and values are non-negative integers
Do not fabricate study data or invent results not supported by the input CSV file
Do not use this skill for continuous outcome data such as mean differences or standardized mean differences

faq.question: What input format does this skill require?
faq.answer: The skill requires a CSV file with columns: study, group1_Events, group1_sample_size, group2_Events, group2_sample_size. An optional outcome_new column can label the outcome measure.

faq.question: What output files are generated?
faq.answer: The skill generates a forest plot PNG image and a CSV data table with odds ratios, confidence intervals, and effect size estimates for each study.

faq.question: Can I use this for continuous data like mean values?
faq.answer: No. This skill only supports binary classification data with event counts and sample sizes. For continuous data, use a different meta-analysis tool.

faq.question: What statistical methods are used?
faq.answer: The R script uses the meta package with the Mantel-Haenszel method. The Python script uses inverse-variance weighted random-effects model.

faq.question: How many studies do I need?
faq.answer: The skill requires at least 2 studies to perform meta-analysis. Fewer than 2 studies will produce an error message.

faq.question: What if the R environment is not available?
faq.answer: The skill automatically falls back to a Python script if the R script fails. Install numpy, pandas, and matplotlib for the Python fallback.
